title: Hawaiian Ho'oponopono and the Anger Cleared
dek: *Anger lives in the body as a clenched fist around old pain. The Hawaiian practice of ho'oponopono offers a doorway out—not through catharsis or positive thinking, but through a humble return to what is broken and a willingness to make it pono, or right. This is the work of reconciliation that changes everything.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Ho'oponopono is a Hawaiian reconciliation practice that clears anger by restoring right relationship with yourself, others, and the spiritual world. The four-phrase protocol works somatically, dropping you beneath reactive stories into the body's held grief and shame. When practiced with presence, it opens a channel where blame dissolves and your nervous system finds the safety it has been seeking.

What if the anger you carry isn't really yours to keep holding? I mean that literally. Not in some airy metaphysical sense, but in the concrete, nervous-system reality that Pat Ogden describes when she talks about how trauma imprints get stored as procedural memory - wordless, embodied, waiting. The clenched jaw you wake with. The heat that floods your chest when someone interrupts you. The way your shoulders climb toward your ears in certain conversations. These aren't character flaws. They're unfinished business written into tissue. Ho'oponopono, the traditional Hawaiian practice of setting things right, works at exactly this level. It's not a gratitude journal or a manifestation technique. It's a reconciliation protocol that our ancestors understood long before we had the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system to prove what they already knew: unresolved conflict lives in the body as a state of permanent vigilance, and the only way out is through repair. The word itself tells you the work. *Ho'o* means "to make." *Pono* means "right" or "balanced" or "in proper relationship." *Ponopono* is the doubling, the emphasis: really right, deeply balanced, restored across all dimensions. When you practice ho'oponopono, you're not just apologizing or forgiving in the casual Western sense. You're entering a ceremonial space where broken relationships - with people, with ancestors, with yourself, with the divine - can be mended at the root. ## The Four Phrases That Dismantle Blame The modern practice, popularized by Morrnah Nalamaku Simeona and later by Dr. Ihaleakala Hew Len, distills the ceremony into four phrases you speak inwardly, repeatedly, with attention: *I'm sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you.* Simple, yes. Simplistic, no. In my own practice, I've watched these phrases crack open defenses I didn't even know I was carrying. The first phrase, "I'm sorry," isn't an admission of fault in the legalistic sense. It's an acknowledgment that you are part of the web, implicated in the suffering, connected to the break. It drops you out of the righteous position and into humility. "Please forgive me" goes deeper. It asks for release - not from the other person necessarily, but from the universe, from the divine intelligence that holds all of it. You're asking to be freed from the karmic knot, the stuck pattern, the loop of blame and counter-blame that keeps the anger alive. "Thank you" is the pivot. Gratitude in the midst of pain is not toxic positivity. It's recognition that the very fact of awareness, of being able to do this work, is itself a gift. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that when we touch suffering with mindfulness, we begin to transform it. The "thank you" is that touch, that willingness to meet what is without turning away. "I love you" completes the circuit. Not as a sentimental add-on, but as the fundamental truth underneath all the conflict: that love is the ground state, the original condition, and everything else is a forgetting. When you say "I love you" to the person who hurt you, to the part of yourself that hurt someone else, to the ancestor whose wound you inherited, you're remembering what was always true. Over the years I've seen people rush through these phrases like a magic spell, trying to get to the other side of their anger without actually feeling it. That's not how it works. Ho'oponopono asks you to stay present while you speak. To notice what arises. To let the body have its response. ## Anger as Unmetabolized Grief Gabor Maté reminds us that anger is often a guardian emotion, standing watch over deeper, more vulnerable feelings we couldn't afford to feel when the original wound happened. A child who can't express rage at a neglectful parent will stuff it down, and twenty years later it erupts at a partner who forgets to text back. The anger feels utterly justified in the moment, but it's carrying a payload from another time. Ho'oponopono works with this layering. When you say "I'm sorry" to your partner about the fight you just had, you might also be speaking to your younger self, to your parent, to the whole lineage of people who never learned how to repair. The practice doesn't ask you to parse all that intellectually. It invites you to stand in the center of the web and speak the phrases into the whole tangle. I remember sitting on my bedroom floor after a vicious argument, chest tight, hands shaking, convinced I was completely in the right. I started the phrases anyway, more out of desperation than faith. "I'm sorry. Please forgive me." The words felt like lies at first. But I kept going, and somewhere around the tenth repetition, my chest cracked open and I was sobbing - not angry-crying, but grieving. Grieving the disconnection. Grieving my own harshness. Grieving the fact that we were both doing the best we could and it still hurt. That's the shift ho'oponopono facilitates. It moves you from the story of who did what to whom, down into the raw bodily truth of suffering and the longing for reconnection. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this theme: the body knows what the mind wants to argue about. When you give the body a pathway to express and release, the mind often follows. ## The Somatic Doorway: Where Forgiveness Lives You can't think your way to forgiveness. You have to feel your way there, and that means working with sensation. When you hold anger in the body, it shows up as constriction - tight throat, shallow breath, clenched gut. Ho'oponopono, practiced with somatic awareness, gives those held places permission to soften. Try this now, if you're willing. Bring to mind a person or situation that still carries a charge of anger for you. Don't pick the hardest one; pick something medium. Notice where you feel it in your body. Throat? Chest? Belly? Jaw? Just notice, without trying to change it. Now place one hand on that area. Say the phrases slowly, out loud or silently: *I'm sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you.* Repeat them five or six times, keeping your attention on the sensation under your hand. Notice if anything shifts - temperature, texture, intensity. Don't force a shift. Just be curious. What you're doing is creating a bridge between the stuck energy and the possibility of flow. Pat Ogden's work in Sensorimotor Psychotherapy shows us that the body holds procedural memories - templates of how to respond to threat - and that these can be updated when we bring mindful awareness to them. Ho'oponopono is one way to do that updating. You're essentially saying to your nervous system: this old response, this held anger, it served a purpose once, but we don't need to keep carrying it. We can set it down now. The phrases aren't magic words that make everything disappear. They're a relational gesture, an opening. In our experience, the real work happens in the space between repetitions, when you pause and breathe and let your body tell you what it needs to say. ## Reconciliation with the Lineage Traditional ho'oponopono wasn't just about interpersonal conflict. It was about restoring harmony across the entire web of relationships, including those with ancestors and descendants. The Hawaiians understood that unresolved pain doesn't die with the body; it gets passed down, encoded in how we parent, how we partner, how we protect or fail to protect. When you practice ho'oponopono, you can direct the phrases not just to the person in front of you, but to the lineage behind you. To the grandmother who survived violence and taught her children to be hard. To the grandfather who drank to numb his shame. To the mother who couldn't give what she never received. "I'm sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you." This isn't about blaming your ancestors or letting yourself off the hook. It's about recognizing that you are a link in a chain, and that you have the power - right now, in this body - to interrupt the transmission of harm. You can be the one who stops passing the anger forward. There's a visceral relief in this. The first time I spoke the phrases to my lineage, I felt something in my upper back release, a knot I'd been carrying for years without knowing it was there. It was as if some part of me had been bracing against the weight of all that inherited pain, and the simple act of acknowledgment - *I see you, I'm sorry, I love you* - allowed me to finally set it down. If you're drawn to work with plant allies in this process, [Banyan Botanicals Vata Massage Oil](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DD8YB7X1?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help ground the practice in the body. Warm a little between your palms and massage it into your feet or belly before you begin. The act of touch, the warmth, the scent - these signal to your nervous system that it's safe to soften. You can explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for other gentle supports. ## The Practice: Five Minutes at the Edge of Sleep Here's a practice you can do tonight, in the liminal space between waking and sleep when the defenses are down and the subconscious is more available. Lie in bed, lights off, body settling. Bring to mind one person you're holding anger toward. Don't rehearse the story of what they did. Just see their face, say their name silently. Place both hands on your heart. Breathe slowly - four counts in, six counts out. Do this three times. Now begin the phrases, speaking them internally with each exhale: *I'm sorry. Please forgive me. Thank you. I love you.* Let each phrase have its own breath. Don't rush. If your mind wanders into the story, gently bring it back to the words and the sensation of your hands on your chest. Do this for five minutes, or until you feel a shift - a softening, a yawn, a sigh, tears, warmth. Any of these are signs that something is moving. If nothing seems to happen, that's fine too. You're planting seeds. The harvest comes later. When you're done, remove your hands, take one more deep breath, and let yourself drift toward sleep. Don't analyze what happened. Let the work continue in the dream space. ## When the Practice Feels False A common obstacle: the phrases feel like lies. You don't feel sorry. You don't want to forgive. The anger feels too justified, the wound too fresh. This is normal. Ho'oponopono isn't asking you to bypass your anger or pretend it isn't there. It's asking you to hold both the anger and the intention to heal, simultaneously. Karla McLaren, in [The Language of Emotions](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1583949968?tag=spankyspinola-20), talks about anger as a boundary-setting emotion that tells you when something isn't right. That's crucial information. You need to listen to it. But you also need to distinguish between anger that's serving you in the present - "this person is violating my boundary right now and I need to act" - and anger that's a residue from the past, keeping you in a state of chronic activation. Ho'oponopono is for the second kind. It's for the anger you're done with but can't seem to put down. The anger that's become a habit, a groove, an identity. When you speak the phrases into that stuck anger, you're not saying the other person was right or that you were wrong. You're saying: I'm ready to be free of this. I'm ready to stop letting this old wound run my present life. If the phrases still feel false, try this variation: "I'm willing to be sorry. I'm willing to forgive. I'm willing to be grateful. I'm willing to love." Willingness is enough. It opens the door a crack, and that's all the universe needs. You might also find that magnesium helps calm the nervous system enough to do this work without getting flooded. [Momentous Magtein Magnesium L-Threonate, 30 Servings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09WWK4R2C?tag=spankyspinola-20) crosses the blood-brain barrier and can take the edge off the fight-or-flight response, giving you a bit more capacity to stay present with hard feelings. For more context on how we approach these practices, visit [the about page](/about). ## Anger and the Myth of the Separate Self At the deepest level, ho'oponopono confronts the illusion that we are separate selves, boundaried and independent, capable of being purely wronged or purely right. The Hawaiian worldview, like many indigenous cosmologies, sees the self as relational, woven into a fabric of connections that includes not just other people but land, ancestors, elements, gods. When you practice ho'oponopono, you're working from that understanding. The anger you feel toward someone else is also, in some mysterious way, anger toward a part of yourself. The forgiveness you offer them is also forgiveness you offer the universe, the whole web that includes both of you. This isn't metaphorical. It's a description of how consciousness actually works when you drop beneath the story of the separate self. Thich Nhat Hanh used the term "interbeing" to point to this reality. We inter-are. Your suffering and my suffering are not two separate things. When I heal my anger, I'm also healing the field we share. When you practice ho'oponopono for your difficult colleague, you're not just improving your own nervous system regulation - you're shifting the relational field between you, and they will feel it, even if they never know what you did. This is not magical thinking. It's the recognition that we are constantly in subtle communication, body to body, nervous system to nervous system, and that the quality of our inner state radiates outward and affects what returns to us. When you soften toward someone internally, they often soften toward you externally, because the cues you're sending have changed. ## Three Questions Readers Actually Ask ### Does ho'oponopono require belief in Hawaiian spirituality to work? No. The practice is effective at the somatic and psychological level regardless of your cosmology. You can approach it as a nervous system regulation tool, a forgiveness practice, or a prayer - whatever frame allows you to actually do it. That said, there's a depth available when you engage with the practice on its own terms, acknowledging the spiritual dimension of reconciliation. You don't have to convert to anything, but staying curious about the worldview the practice comes from will enrich your experience. ### Can I do ho'oponopono for someone who has died or someone I'll never see again? Yes, absolutely. In fact, this is one of the most powerful applications. You're not doing it to change the other person or to get closure from them. You're doing it to change your own internal relationship to the wound. When you speak the phrases to someone who has died, you're speaking to the internalized version of them, the representation you carry in your psyche. That representation can shift, soften, release its grip - and when it does, you're free in a new way. The same applies to people you'll never see again. The work is internal, and its effects are real. ### How long does it take to feel a shift from ho'oponopono practice? This varies wildly. Some people feel an immediate softening, a release of tension, even tears, the first time they speak the phrases with sincerity. Others practice for weeks before they notice a change. The key is consistency and presence. If you're doing it mechanically, rushing through the words, it won't do much. If you're doing it with attention, staying with the sensations and emotions that arise, the practice will work you over time. It's not a one-and-done technique. It's a way of being in relationship with your own reactivity, and that relationship deepens the more you tend it. ## The Anger That Becomes Medicine There's a story the Hawaiian healers tell, about how the very thing that poisons you can also cure you if you know how to work with it. Anger is like that. Left unattended, it calcifies into bitterness, resentment, chronic inflammation, a life organized around who wronged you and how. But met with presence, with the willingness to feel it fully and let it move, anger becomes a fierce clarifying force. It shows you where your boundaries need to be. It reveals what matters to you. It burns away what's false. Ho'oponopono doesn't erase your anger. It transforms your relationship to it. After you've done the practice for a while, you might notice that anger still arises - of course it does, you're human - but it doesn't stick the same way. It moves through faster. You're less interested in rehearsing the story of who did what. You're more interested in what's true right now, in this moment, in this body. That's the real gift of the practice. Not that you become some blissed-out person who never feels anger, but that you become someone who can feel anger without being consumed by it, who can set boundaries without needing the other person to be wrong, who can forgive without condoning harm. You become, in the language of the practice, *pono* - right-sized, balanced, in proper relationship with the whole web. If you're hungry for more practices like this, ones that work at the intersection of body, emotion, and spirit, [more articles in this journal](/articles) explore similar territory. And if you're curious about how these practices weave into a broader understanding of embodied wisdom, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers complementary perspectives on living awake in a world that often prefers we stay numb. The anger you carry has been waiting a long time for you to turn toward it with this kind of gentle, unflinching attention. It's been waiting to be seen, to be felt, to be released back into the elements it came from. Ho'oponopono is one way to do that releasing. Four phrases, spoken with presence, again and again, until the fist in your chest finally opens and you remember what your hands were made for. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on hawaiian ho'oponopono and the anger cleared, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
